title: Enhancing Reliability Using Service-Level Prioritized Load Shedding at Netflix
gist: Netflix engineers detail how they survive extreme legitimate traffic spikes from live/launch events using prioritized load shedding embedded in Envoy sidecars — letting user-initiated requests steal capacity from prefetch traffic — plus a success/failure buffer framework, ORCA-based utilization signals, automated config generation validated by chaos testing, and prioritized retry budgets to prevent retry storms.

themes.theme: The traffic-spike problem and scaling limits

insights.claim: Netflix's Play API sees predictable daily traffic peaking around 7pm US-West and troughing at 3-4am, but popular-title and live launches create massive spikes of unknown magnitude from legitimate (not malicious) users that can exceed provisioned capacity.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Live/event streaming introduces unpredictable legitimate demand spikes that classic capacity planning cannot absorb — a structural reliability challenge as Netflix pushes live content.
insights.locator: My team owns Play API

insights.claim: Neither reactive autoscaling (slow, takes minutes to detect and boot instances) nor proactive scaling (expensive, and cloud capacity may be unavailable) reliably handles outsized thundering-herd spikes like a mass simultaneous rebuffer on a live title.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Autoscaling is structurally insufficient for sub-minute spikes; load shedding is the necessary complement — a design lesson for any spiky-traffic system.
insights.locator: You might have a question, why not just scale up?

insights.claim: Without load shedding, ramping traffic beyond capacity raises latency for all requests (both p50 and p99), piles up threads, and can push a server out of memory or fail health checks, causing cascading failure as unhealthy instances shift pressure to others before autoscaling can add capacity.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Describes the concrete cascading-failure mechanism load shedding exists to prevent — a reliability failure mode to design against.
insights.locator: What happens if there's no load shedding at all?

insights.claim: Load shedding differs from rate limiting: load shedding responds to total request volume exceeding total provisioned capacity, whereas rate limiting enforces a fixed per-user limit and can be used for monetization; the two are complementary.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Clarifies two distinct resilience levers that must be layered together — rate limiting prevents some situations that would otherwise need shedding.
insights.locator: Load shedding is not exactly the same as rate limiting

insights.claim: Congestive failure is the worst case where utilization hits 100%, all requests fail, and the system does not recover even after load drops, requiring a costly node restart; the fix is reserving capacity to gracefully reject requests.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Defines the specific non-recovering failure state that makes load shedding non-optional for high-availability systems.
insights.quote: The system rapidly transitioned from successfully working to failing.
insights.locator: Unfortunately, this system reaches a tipping point

insights.claim: Netflix uses a shared vocabulary of 'success buffer' (extra requests served above baseline without failure) and 'failure buffer' (requests gracefully rejected above the success buffer), because rejecting a request is far cheaper than serving one, so many requests can be rejected for each one not served.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: A reusable, objective vocabulary for reasoning about overload behavior across any load-shedding implementation — adoptable by any platform team.
insights.quote: We can reject many more requests for each one we choose not to serve.
insights.locator: Success buffer and failure buffer give us a generic way

insights.claim: Buffer is expressed as multiples of baseline: a cluster serving 100 RPS baseline that can serve +30 RPS has a 0.3x success buffer and reject +120 RPS has a 1.2x failure buffer (1.5x total), letting teams work backwards from a buffer goal to the baseline traffic a cluster should carry.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Quantifies capacity headroom as a tunable target — the same math as running clusters colder, enabling objective capacity decisions.
insights.locator: How much buffer does this cluster have?

insights.claim: Lowering the baseline traffic dramatically increases buffer: the same cluster at 50 RPS baseline gains a 1.6x success buffer and 2.4x failure buffer for 4x total, and beginning to shed earlier trades success buffer for greatly increased failure buffer.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Explicit trade-off dial: baseline utilization and shed-start thresholds directly govern resilience versus cost.
insights.locator: How do we get more buffer?

insights.claim: A key Netflix insight is that 50% of playback requests were prefetch (browser optimistically fetching titles a user hovers over), whose failure is invisible to users, versus user-initiated requests (actual play) whose failure is user-visible.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Half of load is non-critical and sheddable without user impact — the economic basis that makes prioritized shedding so valuable.
insights.locator: One key insight here is that all requests are not equal

insights.claim: With prioritized load shedding, prefetch availability drops significantly while user-initiated availability can remain at 100% under the same spike, and higher-priority requests are guaranteed higher availability than lower-priority ones.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Shedding by priority converts raw capacity limits into a graceful user-experience-preserving degradation — the core product win.
insights.locator: Can we do better than this?

insights.claim: In a real incident, an infrastructure outage caused Android devices to queue failing playback requests, then upon recovery a huge spike of prefetch retries hit, which prioritized load shedding absorbed while keeping critical-request availability high, preventing delayed outage recovery.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Demonstrates prioritized shedding protecting recovery from misbehaving clients — backend must defend against clients doing the wrong thing.
insights.locator: This is real data. We had a spike in non-critical traffic

insights.claim: During a critical-request spike from a popular live event, critical traffic steals capacity from lower-priority traffic so successful critical RPS rises while lower-priority successful RPS falls, delivering availability ordered by priority (critical > degraded > best-effort).
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Even when all-critical demand exceeds capacity, priority tiers ensure the most important traffic is protected — key for live-event reliability.
insights.locator: In this scenario, we had a critical request spike

insights.claim: For Netflix's Data Gateway fronting databases, writes are prioritized over reads because failed writes cause data loss while reads are retryable, and read RPS is typically higher than write RPS which provides more buffer when shedding begins.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: A transferable prioritization heuristic — protect non-retryable, data-integrity-critical operations first.
insights.locator: We have a service called Data Gateway

insights.claim: Prioritized load shedding beats both no-isolation single clusters and sharded critical/non-critical clusters because the same instance serves both priorities with application-level isolation, letting user-initiated requests steal prefetch capacity during a spike — impossible in a sharded architecture.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Application-level priority isolation gives both isolation and capacity elasticity that physical sharding cannot — an architecture-choice argument.
insights.locator: Let's talk about why prioritized load shedding is better than an alternate solution

insights.claim: Load shedding is modeled as a function taking request priority and current utilization and returning a shed/don't-shed decision; priority should be determined cheaply, best via a request header mapping (so the request body need not be parsed) rather than an expensive remote lookup.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Cheap header-based priority determination avoids spending CPU on requests that will be shed — a critical efficiency design point.
insights.locator: A simple way of thinking about load shedding

insights.claim: Because a malicious actor could inflate their request priority, header-advertised priority must be complemented with per-user rate limiting as a defense layer.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Priority signals are gameable and require rate limiting as a backstop — a security consideration for any priority-based system.
insights.locator: Note that load shedding is not the only line of defense

insights.claim: Netflix uses CPU utilization to detect when a service itself is overloaded and latency/concurrency to detect when a downstream service or database is overloaded, since latency can spike without the local service showing raised CPU.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Multiple utilization signals catch different overload sources — CPU alone misses downstream bottlenecks.
insights.locator: How do we measure utilization?

insights.claim: The latency-based shedder acts as a smarter, progressive timeout that prioritizes user-critical requests, unlike a plain timeout which behaves as a blunt step function.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Reframes load shedding as priority-aware timeout logic — a mental model for evolving timeout strategies.
insights.locator: The latency-based shedder is similar to a timeout

insights.claim: Netflix moved prioritized load shedding from the API gateway to the individual service level because service utilization is a leading indicator (shed before error rates rise), it covers backend-to-backend batch traffic that bypasses the gateway, and it enables local testing within a single service.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Service-level shedding beats gateway-only because gateway error rate is a lagging indicator and misses internal traffic — an architecture evolution lesson.
insights.locator: We have augmented this, and our current architecture

insights.claim: Netflix migrated its load shedding from a Java library to its Envoy-based sidecar proxy, and configures shed thresholds above the autoscaling target — e.g. autoscale at 50% CPU, non-critical shedding at 60%, critical shedding at 80% — so the system scales up while shedding.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: Concrete threshold-stacking config and the Java-to-Envoy sidecar migration are directly reusable reference points.
insights.locator: Our initial implementation was a Java library

insights.claim: With CPU-based prioritized shedding, Netflix handled up to six times the successful RPS on an instance without impacting the latency of successful requests, because CPU growth slows once cheap rejections replace expensive serving past the shed threshold.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: A 6x headroom result quantifies the resilience gain — a benchmark for the technique's payoff.
insights.locator: In this system, we are able to handle, go up to six times

insights.claim: For latency-based utilization Netflix defines normalized utilization as the percent of requests exceeding a latency SLO, e.g. with a 1-second SLO, shed non-critical when 10% of requests exceed 1s and shed critical when 40% exceed 1s.
insights.speaker: Anirudh Mendiratta
insights.so_what: A concrete SLO-percentile-based shedding formula that other teams can adapt to their latency targets.
insights.locator: Another measure we use for utilization is latency

insights.claim: Netflix's Zuul-based API gateway inspects incoming requests via a rules engine (considering the API called and device state) to assign priority, which then follows the request through the entire call graph; services may downgrade priority but never upgrade it, since an upgrade indicates a prioritization bug to fix at the edge.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Edge-assigned, downgrade-only priority propagation is a clean governance model preventing priority inflation deep in the call tree.
insights.locator: Our API gateway, based on the Netflix open-source project, Zuul

insights.claim: Priority propagates as self-propagating headers stored in thread-local (ThreadLocal in Java) lookaside storage that subsequent outgoing calls re-emit, using a Base64-encoded Protobuf header (Contextflow) that is Netflix's generic mechanism for all request metadata, not just priority.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Metadata-propagation infrastructure (Contextflow) is the reusable substrate enabling priority, identity, failure injection, and canary routing across the fleet.
insights.locator: A core capability of our ecosystem is the ability to attach metadata

insights.claim: Netflix validates request priorities experimentally using failure injection testing: failing a high-priority request should impact a tracked metric (e.g. successful next-episode play) while failing a low-priority one should not, with transparent retry so real members aren't harmed during the experiment.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Empirically validating priority assignments prevents mislabeled-critical traffic — many priorities were missing or wrong when they started.
insights.locator: To address this, we designed an experiment to help us validate

insights.claim: Netflix load-sheds different service types at different units: gRPC on the discrete RPC invocation, REST on the incoming request (instrumenting underlying service implementations to avoid path-parameter complexity), and GraphQL by instrumenting individual field resolvers while shedding the entire query or mutation rather than returning partial responses.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: A protocol-by-protocol instrumentation playbook — GraphQL is the hardest case and partial responses are deliberately avoided.
insights.locator: What types of work are these servers performing?

insights.claim: Netflix reasons that if a GraphQL request is low-priority enough to warrant a partial response, it is low-priority enough to shed completely, avoiding wasted resources on responses that may be unusable.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: A resource-efficiency principle: don't partially serve requests you can't confirm are usable.
insights.quote: If the request is already low enough priority to provide a partial response, it's low enough priority for us to shed it completely.
insights.locator: GraphQL also provides a mechanism to provide field-level errors

themes.theme: Utilization aggregation via ORCA and Envoy

insights.claim: Netflix aggregates utilization from multiple sources (RPC latency, and e.g. connected-datastore utilization from the data-platform team) using the open-source ORCA (Open Request Cost Aggregation) message, with each provider writing ORCA files to a shared directory as fully out-of-band load communication with no per-request impact.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: ORCA-based out-of-band load signaling is a standardized, extensible pattern for multi-source utilization aggregation.
insights.locator: Luckily, we have a simple solution in open source

insights.claim: The Envoy-based ingress proxy monitors ORCA files using the Linux kernel inotify API to respond to utilization updates efficiently without polling files for changes.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: A low-overhead kernel-level mechanism for real-time load awareness in the data path.
insights.locator: Our Envoy-based ingress proxy uses an inode notify API

insights.claim: For each cluster and utilization signal, Netflix defines a function mapping (current utilization, request priority) to a shed probability, incrementally shedding lower priorities at 100% before any higher priority is at risk, with a unique per-cluster function because each cluster receives a unique priority mix.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Per-cluster probabilistic shed functions create smooth throttling — the mathematical core of graceful degradation.
insights.locator: For each utilization and for each cluster, we define a function

themes.theme: Automated config generation and chaos validation

insights.claim: Netflix built a system that ingests historical RPC latency, RPC volumes, request priorities, and CPU to auto-generate per-cluster 'resilience configuration' governing expected utilization ranges, shed response functions, and monitoring tuning, so application owners avoid maintaining these values themselves.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Auto-generated resilience config is the leverage that lets a platform team apply load shedding to hundreds of clusters without hand-tuning each.
insights.locator: Being a platform engineer, we built a new system to do this for us

insights.claim: Netflix uses one shared configuration for both load shedding and autoscaling because the information needed to shed effectively is the same needed to scale effectively, with shedding buying time until scale-up completes in the common case.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Unifying shed and scale config reduces operator cognitive load and keeps the two mechanisms coherent.
insights.locator: The information that we need to effectively shed load is the same

insights.claim: Netflix identifies which clusters to onboard using its ChAP chaos automation platform's failure injection testing to measure the impact of a cluster failing, tracing incoming priorities (high-priority inflow signals load-shedding benefit), then asking application owners to validate business-domain impact.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Proactive failure injection prioritizes protection investment before an incident forces it — a mature reliability practice.
insights.locator: Our chaos automation platform, ChAP, helps us here

insights.claim: Netflix auto-validates each generated resilience config by running a ChAP load-spike experiment (e.g. warming clusters to baseline then routing a 4x total / 3x increased spike to a canary), promoting passing configs to production and flagging failures for human review, with automatic shutdown of all tests if any harm is detected.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Closed-loop generate-test-promote automation is what makes robot-reconfigured load shedding safe at fleet scale.
insights.locator: For each cluster, we need to run the load shedding experiment

insights.claim: Netflix schedules load tests to run at most one per region at a time, only when the cluster has enough routed traffic (some clusters see it only a few hours a day and at different times per region), and preferably within the on-call engineer's working hours to avoid paging people awake.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Operational scheduling constraints are as important as the technique itself for safely testing in production.
insights.locator: Deciding when to run these tests looks a lot like a homework problem

insights.claim: Netflix ships a passing load-test config to production as an automated pull request into the application's codebase so it deploys through normal test/canary/rollout validations, keeping the change visible in diffs, attached to a specific build, and free of any special process-initiated rollback.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Treating resilience config as versioned code in the normal deploy pipeline is a governance best practice for auto-generated changes.
insights.locator: Once we're confident that a new configuration will effectively protect

insights.claim: Netflix's first productization attempt failed: packaging resilience config into an ever-incrementing dependency inside a ZIP artifact on the app image made changes hard to observe, hard to associate with a bad build, and hard for engineers to view, so they moved config into a plain file in the application repository.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Visibility and build-traceability of auto-managed config matter more than seamless invisibility — a hard-won productization lesson.
insights.quote: I didn't consider the visibility of this to my application owners.
insights.locator: I want to admit, I didn't get this right on my first attempt

insights.claim: Netflix runs a continuous validation campaign flagging any system not demonstrating its expected buffer for review and risk mitigation, especially before high-profile content releases, because services aren't static and teams continuously add features that shift performance profiles.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Resilience config decays as code evolves; continuous re-validation is required, not one-time setup.
insights.locator: Like I mentioned at the start of the talk, we don't just do this once

insights.claim: Netflix warns on-call engineers before automated production load tests via SlackOps notifications and an abort button, because a sudden spike in load shedding is exactly what alerts are meant to flag and surprising teams with pages is counterproductive.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Coordinating chaos tests with human operators via ChatOps is a practical guardrail for testing in production.
insights.quote: I highly recommend that you do not run automated load tests against your production backends without first warning your on-call engineers.
insights.locator: I highly recommend that you do not run automated load tests

themes.theme: Retry storms and prioritized attempt budgets

insights.claim: Client retries against an overloaded, shedding cluster create retry storms; even single-layer retries can double traffic to a failing cluster, and multi-layer retries produce exponential growth, wasting the already-constrained resources needed to reject calls.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Load shedding alone can be defeated by retry amplification — client-side backoff is a mandatory companion.
insights.locator: What happens when servers begin to reject requests?

insights.claim: Netflix's 'prioritized backoff' overrides retry behavior during load shedding so a shed low-priority call is not retried and instead fails the chain or serves a degraded fallback, though this is imperfect since a retry to a different healthy node might have succeeded.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Suppressing retries for shed low-priority calls conserves scarce reject capacity but trades away recoverable single-node failures.
insights.locator: An easy solution is to just override the backoff behavior

insights.claim: Netflix's 'prioritized attempt budget' computes the ratio of retries to initial attempts from each client node to a cluster: a low ratio allows all retries (likely a single bad node), and as the ratio rises it progressively throttles retries starting with low-priority calls, extending the SRE-book client-side throttling technique with request priorities.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Effectively client-side load shedding — the retry/attempt ratio is a self-contained signal requiring no central control plane.
insights.locator: Our innovation here is something we call a prioritized attempt budget

insights.claim: Prioritized attempt budgets require no live updates from a centralized control plane, operating entirely from configuration available at application startup and implemented in Netflix's service mesh proxy alongside load shedding.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Control-plane-free client resilience avoids a dependency that could itself fail during an incident — a robustness advantage.
insights.locator: Another advantage of this approach is that it doesn't require any live updates

insights.claim: Netflix deliberately designs utilizations to be independent of request complexity (e.g. instrumenting GraphQL data fetchers rather than top-level requests), assuming all requests are single-cost while handling differing priorities by tracking multiple points on the latency distribution, with the key requirement that utilization be monotonically increasing.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Avoiding per-request cost modeling simplifies the system while monotonic utilization keeps the shed function well-behaved — a pragmatic design trade-off.
insights.quote: The really important thing is that your utilization needs to be monotonically increasing
insights.locator: We designed our utilizations to be independent of the request complexity

insights.claim: Netflix abandoned years of tracking per-service latency targets because shifting call patterns during load spikes caused both over- and under-response, concluding that even per-service latency targets impose more configuration than platform customers should bear.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Fedorka
insights.so_what: Static per-service latency targets are brittle under spikes — auto-derived configuration is the better path for a platform team.
insights.locator: we tried for years to track latency targets on a per-service level
